ocr.text: The following data table presents a comprehensive overview of participants involved in a recent study focused on healthcare
professionals across various specialties and sectors. By examining key metrics such as age range and work experience, we aim
to elucidate the diverse backgrounds and qualifications of these practitioners. The data illustrates that a significant proportion of
participants, categorized by the "Age range (in years)," fall within the 40-45 age bracket, indicating a wealth of experience and
maturity within the workforce. Additionally, the "Type of health sector" column highlights the distribution of professionals
among public and private sectors, shedding light on potential variances in practice environments and patient demographics.
Participant code  Age range (in years)  Specialty  Type of health sector  Work experience (in years)
P1  40-45  Specialist physician  Private  15
P2  40-45  Specialist physician  Private  13
P3  60-65  Specialist physician  Public and private  36
P4  60-65  General physician  Public and private  37
P5  30-35  General physician  Public and private  11
P6  35-40  Pediatrician  Private  12
P7  35-40  Pediatrician  Private  10
P8  55-60  ENT surgeon  Private  27
P9  40-45  ENT surgeon  Private  13
P10  50-55  Gynecologist  Private  26
P11  45-50  Gynecologist  Private  20
P12  40-45  Lung and chest diseases specialist  Private  15
P13  55-60  Surgeon  Private  30
P14  55-60  Orthopedic surgeon  Public and private  24
P15  55-60  Surgeon  Public  23
For instance, participant P1, a specialist physician with 15 years of work experience, represents the private health sector. This
specific example underscores the critical role that experienced physicians play in delivering specialized care, particularly in
environments where resources and patient loads can differ markedly from public institutions. By analyzing these factors, the
study aims to draw meaningful correlations between professional experience, sector engagement, and overall effectiveness in
healthcare delivery, ultimately contributing to the ongoing discourse on workforce development in the health sector.
